exercises.prompt: For each scenario, which branch of physics explains it? Put them in the right group.
exercises.solution: Match: A ball bouncing → Classical Mechanics. Water boiling → Thermodynamics. A rainbow → Waves & Optics. An electromagnet → Electricity & Magnetism. An atom's energy levels → Quantum Physics.

exercises.prompt: Your car engine gets hot as it runs. Which branch of physics explains why?
exercises.solution: Thermodynamics explains this. The fuel burns (releasing energy), creating heat and pressure that pushes pistons. Heat is energy moving, which thermodynamics studies.

exercises.prompt: Think about today. List 2 things that classical mechanics explains and 2 things that a different branch explains.
exercises.solution: Classical: Walking (motion), throwing something (forces). Other branches: Phone light (optics), battery power (electricity), cooking (thermodynamics). Pick any 4 from daily life!
hints: Classical: anything moving or changing direction

exercises.prompt: Why do you think physicists divided physics into branches instead of keeping it all as one subject?
exercises.solution: It's easier to master one branch deeply than learn everything at once. Each branch has its own rules, tools, and math. A thermodynamics expert doesn't need all of quantum mechanics to do their job well.
hints: Think about how hard it would be to learn everything at once
Each branch has different tools and rules
Is it easier to be an expert in one area or know a little about everything?
